hypothesis: When the GraphSAGE framework is extended to three-or-more-component gas mixtures (e.g., Ar/O₂/CF₄), using higher-order intermediate layer representations—specifically those from the penultimate hidden layer of each pre-trained single-element network as node features—will yield significantly better predictive accuracy than using either output-layer features or first-layer features, because the penultimate layer encodes chemistry-specific nonlinear interactions at an abstraction level that scales more favorably with the combinatorial complexity introduced by additional mixture components, and this advantage will be measurable as a statistically significant improvement in R² relative to a direct feedforward baseline trained on mixture data of equivalent size.
